coredata.dc:title: Functionalizing Fe3O4@SiO2 with a novel mercaptobenzothiazole derivative: Application to trace fluorometric and colorimetric detection of Fe3+ in water
coredata.dc:creator: Mir, Noshin
Karimi, Pouya
Castano, Carlos E.
Norouzi, Nazgol
Rojas, Jessika V.
Mohammadi, Reza
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent nanocomposite based on 6-amino-2-mercaptobenzothiazole (AMBT) was synthesized, characterized, and used as high-performance probe for detection of trace amounts of Fe3+ ions in water. The prepared AMBT-based ligand was grafted on Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposites, which provide both high surface area for hosting the ligand and easy magnetic removal of the nanoprobe from water. The prepared nanoprobe was characterized by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, thermal gravimetric analysis, and vibrating sample magnetometer. Moreover, the designed nanocomposite showed spectroscopic colorimetric properties against low concentrations of Fe3+ ions in water. The detection limits of the probe for fluorometric and colorimetric detection of Fe3+ were 16.0 × 10−9 M and 0.75 × 10−6 M, respectively. Finally, the performance of the probe was tested in a natural water sample for detecting trace amounts of Fe3+. The theoretical calculations of the molecular structure for the designed organic ligand and its interaction with different ions confirmed strong binding energy of iron atom with thiol group of the ligand.

(a) TEM image of MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposites (inset: TEM image of Fe3O4 nanoparticles before coating with SiO2) (b) XRD patterns of bottom: Fe3O4 nanoparticles (JCPDS ref. code: 19–629), top: MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposites. Note that the wide peaks in both patterns show small crystalline size of the sample.

TGA curves of (a) Fe3O4 (b) Fe3O4@SiO2 and (c) MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2. The total weight losses are 13.81%, 15.33% and 34.66% for (a), (b), and (c), respectively. The higher percentage of weight loss in (c) shows that 0.37 mmol/g of MBTBA ligand is grafted on Fe3O4@SiO2.
N2 Adsorption isotherms of (a) Fe3O4@SiO2 and (b) MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2, Insets: the pore size distributions in the samples.

Testing the fluorescence detecting performance of MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposites against 2.5 × 10−5 M of some metal ions (λexc = 350 nm). It is clear that Fe3+ is the most effective ion in quenching fluorescence emission of the nanocomposite, FR: Free receptor.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra (λexc = 350 nm) of MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposites by titration with Fe3+ buffer solution (0–3 mM). inset: Visual fluorescent color change for MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposite, (A) before and (B) after addition of 500 μM of Fe3+ solution (b) The calibration curve of fluorescence emission of MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposites at 448 nm with increasing Fe3+ (0–3 mM), inset: The linear range of the probe fluorescence performance.

Metal ion (Mn+) concentrations 500 μM, λmax = 448 nm, λex = 350 nm, FR: free receptor.

(b) The calibration curve of fluorescence emission of MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposites in the natural water sample from James River at 448 nm with increasing Fe3+ (715 nM-1.31 mM), inset: The linear range of the probe fluorescence performance.

Synthetic procedure of MBTBA-APTES intermediate and its grafting on Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles.
(a) Chemical structure of MBTBA and (b) molecular orbital structures of HOMO and LUMO of different bonding modes with Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Functionalizing Fe3O4@SiO2 with a novel mercaptobenzothiazole derivative: Application to trace fluorometric and colorimetric detection of Fe3+ in water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent nanocomposite based on 6-amino-2-mercaptobenzothiazole (AMBT) was synthesized, characterized, and used as high-performance probe for detection of trace amounts of Fe3+ ions in water. The prepared AMBT-based ligand was grafted on Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposites, which provide both high surface area for hosting the ligand and easy magnetic removal of the nanoprobe from water. The prepared nanoprobe was characterized by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, thermal gravimetric analysis, and vibrating sample magnetometer. Moreover, the designed nanocomposite showed spectroscopic colorimetric properties against low concentrations of Fe3+ ions in water. The detection limits of the probe for fluorometric and colorimetric detection of Fe3+ were 16.0 × 10−9 M and 0.75 × 10−6 M, respectively. Finally, the performance of the probe was tested in a natural water sample for detecting trace amounts of Fe3+. The theoretical calculations of the molecular structure for the designed organic ligand and its interaction with different ions confirmed strong binding energy of iron atom with thiol group of the ligand.

ce:section.ce:para: Trace amounts of Fe3+ play an important role in biological and chemical processes such as cellular metabolism, oxygen carrying, DNA synthesis, and enzymatic reactions. Iron deficiency leads to anemia and diabetes as well as liver and kidney damages. In less catastrophic scenarios, this deficiency can cause tiredness, headaches, and loss of focus [1]. Metals like iron, may enter the natural waters by different ways including weathering [2], waste products [3], corrosion of metals [4], etc. Therefore, determination of iron content in water resources is of great importance. Moreover, mapping the distribution of iron in natural waters, particularly in trace amounts, needs a highly sensitive tool able to detect concentrations as low as in the nanomolar range. On the other hand, because of ubiquitous environmental chemicals and contaminants, it is essential to design a highly selective sensor for the target ion [5].

In recent years, exploiting chemical sensors for selective detection of different materials in gas, liquid, or solid phases has attracted great interest [6–9]. It has been previously shown that the stability constant of the complex is an important factor in the selective detection of metal in a waste solution [10,11]. It is well known that charge, radius, and electronic structure of the metal and the donor atom of the ligand determine the complex stability constant [12]. Therefore, the binding energy of metal-ligand can be representative of this critical parameter. Moreover, naked-eye detection of the analyte by the aid of newly designed chemical and nanostructured probes has been introduced as an alternative for sophisticated analytical methods such as atomic absorption spectroscopy [13], voltammetry [14], and inductively coupled plasma -mass and -optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-MS and ICP-OES, respectively) [15], which are accompanied by tedious preconcentration and digestion procedures as well as spectral interferences [16,17]. ICP spectroscopy is one of the most sensitive known methods among them. The reported detection limit (DL) and qualification limit (QL) are 90 × 10−9 M and 272 × 10−9 M for ICP-OES and 0.90 × 10−9 M and 2.72 × 10−9 M for ICP-MS methods, respectively [18,19]. Although these methods are very sensitive and reliable, well-designed chemical sensors are able to go even further; as an example, recently there has been significant progress in devising a novel Fe3+ fluorescent sensor with high sensitivity of 10−12 M [20]. Moreover, Singh and Mishra showed that the performance and the obtained slope of the calibration curve of a fluorescent carbon sensor for detection of Fe3+ are comparable with that of ICP-EOS [21]. The fluorescent organic chemicals and their capability for selective coordination to different metal ions have spurred much research in developing fluorescent probes. Due to some problems associated with organic fluorescent ligands, including homogeneity and difficult separation, these counterparts have been deposited on the surface of magnetic Fe3O4@SiO2 core-shell nanocomposites for easy magnetic separation after each usage [22–24]. The resulting product is a low-cost, clean, simple, separable, and portable probe that minimizes the use of costly fluorescent ligands.

Applications of fluorescent ligand-functionalized Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposites have so far been reported for various metal ions and biomolecules [25–29]. On the other hand, ferric ion fluorescent detection by Fe3O4@SiO2 sensors has been rarely studied [30] and has been mainly performed on the basis of free organic chemosensors [31–37], nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots [38–42], ligand-functionalized graphene quantum dots [43–47], dendrimer-based sensors [48], functionalized gold nanoparticles [49], and carbon dots [50–55].

Besides fluorescent detection of ions, qualitative and quantitative colorimetric detection has also been widely investigated. Different colorimetric probes have been reported for Fe3+ detection in water, most of which are platforms grafted on gold nanoparticles (NPs) and rely on surface plasmon resonance (SPR) spectral shift of the substrate [56–58]. However, there are not many reports on colorimetric detection of Fe3+ by chemosensors or other nanocomposites [59,60].

The 6-amino-2-mercaptobenzothiazole (AMBT) is a chemical from 2-mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT) family which are conventionally used in the vulcanization of rubber [61]. Harvey et al. showed that AMBT is a potential corrosion inhibitor for AA2024-T3 and AA7075-T6 aluminum alloys, in which its low solubility is comparable with that of chromate, a well-known corrosion inhibitor [62]. They showed that the amino group in this ligand makes it a compound with low solubility which is of particular interest for water treatment purpose because even if not attached to a surface, it can be easily removed by a filter paper. AMBT has also been employed in functionalization of gold nanoparticles for dopamine detection in pharmaceutical samples [63]. Having an amine head and a thiol tail makes this unique ligand a group for facile grafting on nanocomposite from its head and coordinating to metal ion by its tail. Moreover, adding an aromatic spacer such as terephthalaldehyde (TP) can add fluorescence properties to its profile.

We have already reported a number of fluorescent probes for the detection of heavy metal ions in water [64–66]. In continuation of the previous work, here we report synthesis and characterization of a new fluorescent functionalized-Fe3O4@SiO2 based on AMBT ligand. This molecule was selected because it is very promising for improving the surface engineering of a potential nanocomposite for metal ion detection in water. While its special chemical structure provides both grafting on the surface and metal removal from water, the reduced activity of thiol group which is resulted from the electron withdrawal of the nitrogen in the ring [62] warranties a selective metal ion extraction. The introduced probe shows very high sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+ ions in water. The possible competing ions are determined from the theoretical calculation of metal-ligand binding energy. It is shown that the most competing ions for Fe3+ adsorption to the designed probe are Cu2+ > Ni2+ > Hg2+ > Zn2+ from which the most competitive one (Cu2+) has the M-S binding energy almost 2.7 times weaker than Fe3+. Moreover, the probe exhibits colorimetric properties with good sensitivity and wide linear range. The designed probe could be further developed for trace detection of ferric ions in low iron concentrated natural water resources.
The details of materials and characterization information could be found in supplementary material.

The 4-(((2-mercaptobenzo[d]thiazol-5-yl)imino)methyl)benzaldehyde (MBTBA) Schiff base was synthesized by reacting AMBT ligand with TP. Briefly, 30 mL of 0.25 M AMBT ligand in ethanol: dry DMF (70:30) solution was prepared at 80 °C and to this solution, 20 mL of 0.25 M ethanol solution of TP containing 0.5 mL acetic acid was added dropwise. In order to remove the produced water during the Schiff base reaction, prevent the reverse direction in the equilibrium, and achieve high yield, 0.5 g 3A molecular sieve was also added to the flask. The resulting mixture was stirred and heated at 80 °C for 4 h. The reaction is shown in Scheme 1 . Then, the yellow precipitate was filtered off and washed with ethanol several times to remove the unreacted substances. After drying at 80 °C, 0.5 mmol of MBTBA was dissolved in 30 mL dry DMF containing 0.5 g 3A molecular sieve. Then, 0.5 mmol (0.117 mL) APTES was added to this solution at 60 °C. After 2 h, the solution was placed in the ice bath for 2 h and the orange product was filtered off, washed, and recrystallized in dry DMF.

For Fe3O4 nanoparticles synthesis, a solvothermal process already reported by Si et al. [67] was used. Briefly, 4 mmol FeCl3 salt was dissolved in n-hexane solution containing 40 mmol laurylamine and 12 mmol oleic acid. Then, 2 mmol iron powder was added to the solution and the resulting mixture was put into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 180 °C for 24 h. The resulting black precipitate was collected by centrifuge and dried at 80 °C overnight. The coating procedure of Fe3O4 NPs with SiO2 was conducted according to the method reported by Zhu et al. [68]. Shortly, 25 mg of the as-prepared Fe3O4 NPs was added to 10 mL cyclohexane followed by ultrasonic dispersion. Then, 1.8 g Triton X100, 1.6 mL hexanol, and 0.34 mL H2O were added and the solution was vigorously stirred for 15 min. Next, 40 mL TEOS was added to the solution and stirring was continued for 30 min. Finally, 0.1 mL aqueous ammonia (28–30%) was added and the mixture was stirred for 24 h to form Fe3O4@SiO2. The formed nanocomposite was isolated by centrifugation and washed with ethanol, then was put in the oven at 80 °C to dry.

To graft MBTBA ligand on Fe3O4@SiO2 surface, 0.1 g of the nanocomposite was added to 30 mL dry DMF and completely dispersed by ultrasonic waves for 10 min. Then, 1 mmol of the prepared MBTBA ligand was added to this solution. The mixture was refluxed for 12 h at 60 °C and finally, the light-yellow product was collected by centrifuge and left to dry at 80 °C. The overall process starting from AMBT to synthesize MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 is shown in Scheme 1 and Scheme 2 .

To test the fluorometric detection ability of the designed nanoprobe, metal ion solutions were prepared in 10 mM HEPES buffer solution (50,50 H2O:CH3CN, pH = 7.0). Next, 0.002 g of the prepared MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposite was dispersed in 2 mL of the buffer solution and after recording the original fluorescence of the free receptor (FR), the required amounts of different metal ion solutions were added to this solution to have 1 × 10−6 M of different metal ions. The nitrate or chloride salts of Hg2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ag+, and Fe3+ ions were selected to be tested for fluorometric detection. After finding the target ion by experimental measurements, the titration experiment was conducted on the detected ion. The response of the nanoprobe to the target ion was also examined in a river water sample collected from James River, Richmond, VA, USA. The DL was calculated by using Eq. (1) [69]:
(1)
DL =
F × SD
b
where the F factor is 3.3 for DL measurements [70–72], SD is the residual standard deviation of the linear regression, and b is the slope of the regression line.

The molecular structure of MBTBA ligand and its interaction with Fe3+ and the other tested ions in the ground state were optimized based on density functional theory (DFT) at the M06-2X/SDD level of theory using Gaussian09 commercial package [73]. Also, all structures and molecular orbitals were visualized by means of GaussView 5.0.8 [74].

The functional groups and bond characterization of the samples were determined by FT-IR spectroscopy. FT-IR spectra of MBTBA ligand, Fe3O4 NPs, Fe3O4@SiO2, and MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2, are shown in Fig. 1a-d, respectively. In Fig. 1a, two peaks at 3017 and 3088 cm−1 are attributed to CH of the aromatic rings. The absence of the peak at 3320 cm−1 assigned to NH2 group of 6-amino-2-mercaptobenzothiazole shows that the Schiff base ligand is formed by the condensation reaction of NH2 and CO groups of the reactants. The peaks that appeared at 2724 and 2851 cm−1 belong to CH stretching vibration and the band at 1694 cm−1 is assigned to CO stretching vibration of the aldehyde group of the MBTBA ligand. The weak peak at 1652 cm−1 is associated with imino (C=N) stretching absorption of the Schiff base ligand and two peaks at 1580and 1613 cm−1 are ascribed to the aromatic CC in-ring stretching [75]. The peaks at 419 and 1030–980cm−1 are for aromatic CH out-of-plane bending vibrations and the bands observed at 1067, 1130, 1187, 1221, and 1249 cm−1 are assigned to CH in-plane bending vibrations. The highly polarizable SH and CS bonds of benzothiazole ring should appear with strong peaks in the IR spectrum. SH spectrum is expected in the region 2600–2550 cm−1; however, as already reported in the literature [76], this vibration does not usually appear in experimental IR and herein, the only observed peak is a weak peak at 2652 cm−1. The CS stretching vibration absorbs representative peaks at 608 and 720 cm−1. The aromatic CN on benzothiazole ring absorbs strongly at 1330–1260cm−1 [77], which herein has appeared at 1320 cm−1. In Fig. 1b the FT-IR spectrum for Fe3O4 NPs is shown. According to the literature, magnetite shows two absorptions at 390 and 570 cm−1, which are attributed to FeO stretching modes [78]. Since the one at 390 cm−1 is out of the detection range of mid-IR, only the second peak is detectable here, which has appeared at 545 cm−1. Other weak peaks are ascribed to the remained slight amounts of surfactant and solvents. In Fig. 1c-d the FT-IR spectra for Fe3O4@SiO2, and MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 are shown, respectively. In both spectra, the peak at ca. 1052 cm−1 is assigned to Si–O–Si stretching vibration and the strong absorption at ca. 440 is related to SiO out-of-plane deformation. The absorptions at 796 and 952 cm−1 are assigned to SiO bending and Si–OH stretching vibrations, respectively. The weak peaks at 554 in both samples are attributed to the Fe3O4 core and the wide peak at ca. 3260cm−1 is associated with OH vibration. Although both spectra have the mentioned peaks in common, Fig. 1d shows some weak peaks corresponding to MBTBA ligand. The appeared weak peaks at 662, 691, 1298, 1387, 1439, and 1568 cm−1 are all available in Fig. 1a at lower wavenumbers. The decreasing frequency of the peaks is because of grafting the ligand on the SiO2 surface [79]. The peaks at 2870 and 2931 cm−1 in MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 sample (Fig. 1d) are assigned to sp3 CH stretching of alkane chain of APTES molecule on SiO2 surface. The absorption at 1660cm−1 is attributed to (C=N) stretching absorption of imino groups in the grafted ligand. Overall, FT-IR spectroscopy confirms step-by-step formation of MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 product.

The TEM image of MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposites is shown in Fig. 2a. The inset shows the Fe3O4 core before the SiO2 coating. The size of Fe3O4 nanoparticles is in the range of 7–27 nm and the size of MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposites was observed to be in the range of 15–35 nm with the shell thickness of 7–20 nm. The particles in both samples are partly agglomerated because of the magnetic nature of the core. The phase formation of both samples was confirmed by XRD. In Fig. 2b, bottom and top, the XRD patterns of Fe3O4 and MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles are shown, respectively. The pattern in Fig. 2b, bottom, corresponds to the cubic phase of Fe3O4 with JCPDS Card No.: 19–629. In Fig. 2b, top, the broad hump at 2θ = 20–30° is attributed to the amorphous silica and the weak crystalline peaks of Fe3O4 confirms the presence of cores. The wide peaks in both samples show decreased crystalline domain sizes. The calculated Fe3O4 particle size using Scherrer's equation [80] was 26.94 nm which is almost in agreement with TEM analysis.

TGA thermograms for Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, and MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 are shown in Fig. 3a-c, respectively. The weight losses from 30 to 200 °C in all samples are related to endothermic physical water loss from the surface of the samples [81]. In Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@SiO2 samples (Fig. 3a and b, respectively), the second step is associated with the loss of the structural water which confirms higher content of water in the latter sample due to the presence of SiO2 layer [82]. The combustion of organic residuals occurs at 300–500 °C, which obviously shows much larger weight loss in MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 compared with two other samples. This is attributed to combustion of surface grafted MBTBA-APTES ligand. The total weight losses are 13.81%, 15.33% and 34.66% for Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, and MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 samples, respectively. Comparing Fig. 3b and c show that the grafted organic ligand to Fe3O4@SiO2 is about 14.87 wt%, which shows that about 0.37 mmol/g of the ligand is available on the support.

The N2 adsorption isotherms for Fe3O4@SiO2 and MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 samples are shown in Fig. 4a and b, respectively. The BET specific surface areas were calculated to be 110.03 and 114.60m2/g, for bare and functionalized Fe3O4@SiO2, respectively. Moreover, the insets in Fig. 4 represent the pore size distributions in the samples. As can be seen, bare Fe3O4@SiO2 (Fig. 4a, inset) contains a wide range of pore sizes from 2.23 to 4.88 nm ending up in a cumulative pore volume of 0.10 cm3/g. However, in MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 sample (Fig. 4b, inset), the pore size distribution is mainly limited to the sizes of 2.02 and 2.45 nm with a cumulative pore volume of 0.16 cm3/g. The obtained results show that with functionalizing the sample, both surface area and pore volume have slightly increased. On the other hand, the large pores have been replaced with tiny ones. It is well known that organic molecules and polymers can be used in porous materials to partition pore spaces in order to achieve intricate designs with small pores for specific purposes like high-performance adsorptions [83–85]. Therefore, vanishing large pores in the bare sample shows that the ligands have covered them via chemical binding leaving tiny spaces. These new holes probably have created additional traps for N2, resulting in a slight increase of the surface area.

In Fig. 5a the magnetic properties of MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 measured by VSM at 300 K is shown. The diagram shows that the saturation magnetization (Ms) is 0.72 emu/g. The zero amount of the coercivity, which is shown in higher magnification in the inset of Fig. 5a, demonstrates that the nanocomposite has superparamagnetic properties [86]. The magnetic separation of the prepared nanocomposite in Fig. 5b confirms the separation of the material after about 5 min by placing a magnet. This shows that the prepared product is suitable for efficient detection of the metal with easy separation after the use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of metal ions by MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The designed magnetic fluorescent probe, MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2, was tested for its performance against various metal ions. In Fig. 6 the fluorescence behavior of the probe by adding 2.5 × 10−5 M solutions of different metal ions was shown. The FR was excited at λexc = 350 nm and the λmax emission was recorded at 448 nm. As can be observed, metal ions including Ag+, Cd2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and Fe3+ were tested for fluorescence detection. After 3 min of incubation time, a significant fluorescence quenching was observed only when Fe3+ was added to the solution containing the FR. Except for Zn2+, which had a small effect, other metals had no significant influence on decreasing fluorescence emission of the probe. While the fluorescence intensity was not tuned more than 200units for all other metal ions, Fe3+ caused around 1600 units decrease in intensity. Therefore, MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 was considered as an effective fluorescence probe for Fe3+ detection in water.

The titration test for detection of Fe3+ in HEPES buffer solution (50:50 H2O:CH3CN, pH = 7.0) is shown in Fig. 7 . To perform the test, first 0.002 g of MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposite was dispersed by using ultrasonic bath in 2 mL of buffer solution, and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded. Then, different Fe3+ concentrations were added gradually and after each addition, the spectrum was recorded. The final concentrations of the tested solution were 0, 1.5, 15, 30, 40, 55, 110, 185, 285, and 450 × 10−9 M as low concentrations and 0.0035, 0.005, 0.015, 0.065, 0.15, 0.45, 0.75, 1, 1.3, 1.6, 2, 2.3, 2.5, 2.6, and 3 × 10−3 M as high concentrations. In Fig. 7a the fluorescence spectrum for titration test is displayed and it shows that by increasing the concentration of Fe3+, all four fluorescence peaks at 424, 448,485, and 533 nm are gradually quenching. Therefore, the designed probe shows a fluorescence turn-off mechanism towards Fe3+ ions. The inset in Fig. 7a shows the visual fluorescent color change for MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposite, before (Fig. 7a-A) and after (Fig. 7a-B) the addition of 500 μM of Fe3+ solution. It is seen that Fe3+has caused distinct fluorescence quenching and color changes under UV light.

In Fig. 7b the dependence of the intensity of fluorescent emission of MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposite to Fe3+ concentration at λmax = 448 nm is shown. As shown in the inset, a good linear relationship is observed in the range of 1.5 × 10−9 –1.10 × 10−7 M of Fe3+. The regression equation was obtained to be F = -390.73C(mM Fe3+) + 0.9979 and the R2 of the linear trend line was 0.9872. The DL was calculated to be 16.0 × 10−9 M. The obtained high sensitivity shows that the designed probe is promising for quantitative detection of trace amounts of Fe3+ for applications in which accurate measurements are required. Moreover, the proposed nanocomposite shows high sensitivity with respect to other reported solution-based probes, Table 1 summarizes various reports.

The competition of other metals with Fe3+ for affecting fluorescent behavior of the probe was also determined and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . The tested interfering ions including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and Ag+ (500 μM) were added to the FR solution in absence of Fe3+ ions and then equal equivalent of Fe3+ was added. The measured fluorescence spectrums showed that all the aforementioned metal ions almost have no or only slight interference with Fe3+ ions. In addition, to evaluate the performance of MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe in a more realistic sample, a quantification test using same amounts of Fe3+ ions in water collected from James River, Richmond, VA, USA was performed. Although small interference with the fluorescent activity of the probe was observed, it can be seen that after the addition of Fe3+ solution, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity is significantly decreased. The fluorescence quenching degree for the DI-water sample after addition of 500 μM Fe3+ was 67.37% while in water from the river source was 50.19%. The difference could be attributed to the presence of different organic and inorganic species in the river water. These suspensions cause the turbidity of water, expressed in formazin nephelometric units (FNU), and contain the solids which block light pathway in the water. Although most of them do not have fluorescence properties, they are able to scatter or refract the excitation and emission light [87]. The turbidity of the James River water on the time of the collection has been reported to be 3.5 FNU [88]. Thus, it is roughly estimated that the probe can be functional in water samples with turbidity less than 13.7 FNU. Therefore, MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 is suggested as a good candidate for detection of Fe3+ in natural water resources with the low turbidity.

The fluorometric performance of the probe was separately tested in a water sample from James River by titrating the sample with Fe3+ solution. The initial concentration of iron ions in the sample was measured by using ICP-OES to be 7.15 × 10−7 M. The fluorometric experiment was performed using similar conditions to the controlled experiment in DI water but replacing the buffer solution with the natural river water sample. In Fig. 9a the change of the fluorescence emission of the water river sample containing MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe by gradually adding Fe3+ to the solution is shown. It should be mentioned that at the initial concentration of 7.15 × 10−7 M in the river water sample, the intensity of the fluorescence is 1.25-fold higher than the same concentration in the buffer sample. The different performance of the chemical probes in buffer solution and real sample has been previously observed, as well [89]. The final concentrations of Fe3+ added to the testing solutions were 715, 750, 825, and 900 × 10−9 M as low concentrations and 0.0011, 0.0013, 0.0040, 0.0070, 0.0145, 0.0650, 0.160, 0.460, 0.760, and 1.31 × 10−3 M as high concentrations. A similar quenching trend as the one reported in Fig. 7a is also observed in this case. The calibration curve shown in Fig. 9b confirms the quenching behavior of the probe by increasing Fe3+ concentration. A good linear relationship in the range of 7.15–9.0 × 10−7 M is observed in the inset of Fig. 9b. The regression equation of F = -0.0006C(× 10−9 M Fe3+) + 1.4205 and the R2 = 0.9825 was calculated for the best linear line. The DL was calculated to be 43.9 × 10−9 M. The slight difference in DL and quenching behavior of the probe in buffer solution and the river water sample is ascribed to the complex organic species present in the natural water resources. The dissolved organic carbon (DOC) in natural waters have been mainly reported as polymeric organic acids, called humic substances containing carboxylic, hydroxide, and phenolic functional groups, which in fact may slightly interfere with the probe performance [90].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of Fe3+ by using MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to fluorescence quenching, the introduced probe was able to cause distinct spectral change as well as visual color change after adding Fe3+. In Fig. 10a the UV spectral change of MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 with the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ solutions in CH3CN:HEPES buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.0) is shown. Neither FR nor Fe3+-receptor showed a distinct peak at the visible region, and therefore only the UV region is shown. The UV spectrum of the FR shows one distinct peak at 264 nm. With increasing Fe3+ solution, on one hand, this peak becomes stronger, and on the other hand, a new wide peak is growing at 298 nm that gradually becomes stronger and wider. This strong peak at UV region is attributed to metal-to-ligand charge transfer that is responsible for the deep yellow color of the solution after addition of Fe3+ [59,60,91]. The change of the absorbance against Fe3+ concentration is shown in Fig. 10b and in its inset, the real visualized color change with increasing Fe3+ is observed. The calculated DL for this method is 0.75 × 10−6 M, which is much lower than the environmental protection agency guideline in drinking water (5.37 × 10−6 M). [92]. The linear range of the detection was also calculated and found to be between 0.75 × 10−6 and 100 × 10−6 M with R2 = 0.9995, which is shown in Fig. 10c. The colorimetric platforms reported by Jo et al. [60], Lashgari et al. [93], You et al. [59], Wu et al. [94], and Buduru et al. [56] showed DL of 0.27, 0.56, 0.19, 5.6, 5.83 μM, respectively, which are comparable to the result reported here.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculations of metal ions interactions with MBTBA ligand
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 3a shows the optimized structure of ligand together with numbers of atoms. To better specify the bonding modes of different ions with MBTAB ligand, we computationally studied interactions of the various metal ions with S13-H14 position on the ligand. It should be noted that the Al+3 and Co+2 ions failed to form complexes with ligand computationally due to convergence failure. The binding energy (BE) values of the metal ion-ligand complexes are available in Table 2 . As can be seen, the largest BE value belongs to the interaction of the Fe+3 ions with S13-H14 position. Although thiol group is supposed a very good coordination site for soft metals, its reduced activity in mercaptobenzothiazole ligands has been attributed to the presence of nitrogen in the ring which tends to withdraw electrons from the thiol group, reducing its activity [62].

The interactions of other possible positions of the ligand including S12, N11, N15, and N30 with Fe+3 ion were investigated, as well. The results reveal that no stable complex is formed for the interaction of the Fe+3 ions that directed towards N11 position of the ligand. The BE values (in kcal mol−1) for interactions of the Fe+3 ions with S13-H14, S12, N15, and N30 positions were calculated to be 761.11, 683.90, 751.17, and 758.48, respectively. This difference in binding ability of Fe3+ to different sites of the ligand is also obvious from the visual electron cloud shown in Scheme 3b. Comparing HOMO electron clouds of different sites of the ligand shows that the density of the electron cloud is more localized on S13-Fe3+ bond. Thus, the Fe+3 ions interact preferably with S13-H14 position of the ligand.

Energies of the highest occupied molecular orbital (EHOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (ELUMO) and energy gap (EG) values are gathered in Table 2. The EHOMO, ELUMO, and EG values for the ligand are −7.56, −1.64, and 6.00 eV, respectively. These data show that the interactions of metal ions with MBTAB lead to a decrease of energy gaps of the complexes in comparison with the ligand. In addition, there is an inverse correlation between EHOMO and BE of the formed inorganic complexes. As can be observed in Fig. 11 , a decrease in EHOMO results in an increase in BE.

The atomic charges were calculated to have better insight into the order of BE values. First, the atomic charge on each metal ion in the complex (qion) and variation of charge of the metal ions through interaction with ligand (Δqion) were considered (see Table 2). These data show that the Fe+3 ion has the largest Δqion and forms complex with the largest BE. Also, the sum of the atomic charges on the rings A and B in the complexes (qA+B) and variation of the sum of the atomic charges on these rings in the ligand through interactions with the metal ions (ΔqA+B) were calculated. Sum of the atomic charges on the mentioned rings in the ligand is −1.093 e. Thus, the interactions of the metal ions lead to an increase in the atomic charges on the rings A and B. Moreover, the Fe+3-ligand complex has larger ΔqA+B value than those of the other complexes. Consequently, Δqion and ΔqA+B are two key factors that have important roles in strength of binding in the metal ion-ligand complexes.

In conclusion, the designed MBTBA-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposites successfully detected Fe3+ ions in water with by fluorometric and colorimetric mechanism with detection limits of 16.0 × 10−9 and 0.75 × 10−6 M, respectively. The linear ranges of fluorometric and colorimetric detections were between 1.5 × 10−9–1.10 × 10−7 M and 0.75 × 10−6–100 × 10−6 M, respectively. The performance of the ligand in natural water resource showed that it is capable of sensing Fe3+ on a detection limit of 43.0 × 10−9 M. The theoretical calculations confirmed higher binding energy of Fe ions with S13-H14 position of MBTBA ligand compared to the other metal ions. This high BE was attributed to lower EHOMO as well as higher atomic charges (Δqion and qA+B), for the case of Fe-MBTBA complex compared to those of other metal ions. The new concepts and innovation of this work are employing a very simple ligand (i.e. AMBT) in synthesizing a fluorescent grafting agent for ultra-sensitive detection of a very important metal ion in water. Instead of using commercial expensive fluorescent ligands, a simple Schiff base reaction was employed to prepare an effective fluorescent ligand from two non-fluorescent low-cost reactants. The introduced ligand contains a thiol group with reduced activity, which is very useful for selective detection of metal ions. The synthesized ligand can be readily attached to Fe3O4@SiO2 nanocomposite as a carrier that is easily removed from the water after use by applying a magnetic field. The hypothesis of using a simple and low-cost ligand containing a thiol group and its grafting on a magnetic well-known carrier was the inspiration of this work. Compared with previous works, the designed nanocomposite in this work has higher fluorometric detection limit for Fe3+ in the nanomolar range [31,37,38,43,48,95,96]. In addition to its fluorescence properties, it shows colorimetric sensing ability which is usually found in sensors containing expensive gold nanoparticles [56–58] and its detection limit is comparable with other reported results [56,59,60,93,94]. The future possible works of this product could be defined on two more applications including removal of heavy metals ions from water and cellular metal toxicity assays. Moreover, for environmental purposes, a study could be conducted on samples from different natural water resources to optimize its performance for large scale applications.
